paper_metadata.title: Yellow Vests, Pessimistic Beliefs, and Carbon Tax Aversion
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Representative survey of 3,002 French households (Feb–Mar 2019) plus official Insee household surveys to compute objective incidence and net gains.
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Studies French support/opposition to a carbon tax whose revenues are redistributed via a tax-and-dividend (uniform and targeted) scheme in the Yellow Vests context, and shows causal effects of beliefs about personal incidence and environmental effectiveness on approval.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: belief that one does not lose from it (win/lose category)
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived self-interest: household does not lose purchasing power
determinants.definition: Respondent believes their household would not lose purchasing power (“win vs lose” self-interest belief) from the carbon tax & dividend reform.
determinants.mechanism: Expected personal net benefits reduce perceived costs and increase acceptance/support despite higher energy prices.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax increase recycled as dividends (uniform 110€/adult and income-targeted variants); causal effect estimated using fuzzy RDD/IV around eligibility and feedback.
determinants.outcome_variable: Acceptance of the tax & dividend reform ("Yes" or "Don’t know").
determinants.evidence_quote: the belief that one does not lose from it increases the acceptance rate by more than 50 p.p. (53 p.p. in our main specification).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: IV/fuzzy RDD: coefficient 0.534 => ~+53 p.p. on acceptance (“Yes” or “Don’t know”); Table 5.2.
determinants.notes: Estimated with 2SLS: random dividend eligibility (targeted dividend) and discontinuity in win/lose feedback threshold.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Believing that the tax is effective (environmental effectiveness)
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived environmental effectiveness of the carbon tax & dividend
determinants.definition: Respondent believes the carbon-tax-and-dividend policy would effectively reduce pollution and combat climate change.
determinants.mechanism: Believing the policy will deliver environmental outcomes increases perceived policy legitimacy and willingness to approve.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & dividend reform; 2SLS uses random information treatments as instruments for the belief that the policy is environmentally effective.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of the tax & dividend reform ("Yes").
determinants.evidence_quote: Believing that the tax is effective increases the likelihood of approving it by 42 p.p. (1).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 2SLS (IV): +0.416 => +42 p.p. on approval when believing effectiveness; Table 5.4.
determinants.notes: Environmental effectiveness belief instrumented using random info on scientific consensus and climate change.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Progressivity belief (benefit the poorest / not regressive)
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived progressivity: benefits poorest / not regressive
determinants.definition: Respondent believes the tax & dividend is progressive—poorer households gain and the richest (higher energy consumers) lose purchasing power.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived distributional fairness and protection of low-income households increase acceptance/support for the reform.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Beliefs about progressivity after receiving progressivity information; correlations with acceptance estimated in online Appendix K (no IV identified).
determinants.evidence_quote: the effect of progressivity remains statistically significant and as high as 27 p.p. in our preferred specification.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Non-causal OLS/logit: marginal effect of progressivity belief at mean ≈ +27 p.p. on acceptance after controls.
determinants.notes: Estimated with OLS/logit controlling for motives and covariates; interpreted as partial correlation, not causal.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: WTP from subjective gain (subjective net purchasing-power change)
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Subjective expected net gain (implied WTP threshold)
determinants.definition: Higher subjective expected net gain from the tax & dividend increases acceptance rates and implies a higher willingness to incur losses for support.
determinants.mechanism: People support the policy when expected personal gains exceed a loss tolerance, reflecting self-interest.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance/support analyzed as a function of respondents’ subjective gain (euro ranges), used to infer willingness to pay in Figure L.1.
determinants.outcome_variable: Implied willingness to pay (median loss tolerated) / acceptance as function of subjective gain.
determinants.evidence_quote: this WTP is about 60e/year per c.u. since this corresponds to the subjective loss below when a majority accepts the policy.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Implied WTP ≈ 60 €/year per consumption unit; acceptance threshold corresponds to subjective loss tolerated.
determinants.notes: WTP is computed conditionally on believing the policy’s environmental effectiveness.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: pessimistic bias about net gains (overestimate losses)
determinants.definition: Respondents overestimate the carbon tax & dividend’s negative impact on their own purchasing power (pessimistic subjective net-gain bias).
determinants.mechanism: Overestimating personal costs makes the reform unattractive, shifting attitudes toward opposition to the carbon tax & dividend.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Comparison of subjective vs objective household-specific net gains for the tax & dividend; bias is linked to opposition intensity.
determinants.outcome_variable: Opposition to the tax & dividend reform (disapproval of the reform).
determinants.evidence_quote: the people most opposed to the policy, and in particular those that support the Yellow Vests, are the most biased, i.e., the most inclined to overestimate their losses.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Median gap objective vs subjective gains: 116€; 89% underestimate purchasing-power gains for the tax & dividend.
determinants.notes: Bias defined using household-specific objective net gains computed from official data; stronger negative bias aligns with greater opposition.
